title: Deucravacitinib -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
content: Deucravacitinib is the first Janus kinase (Jak) inhibitor medication found to inhibit tyrosine kinase 2 (Tyk2) selectively. [1] [2] The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved deucravacitinib on September 9th, 2022, as a treatment for adult patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis, provided these patients are candidates for systemic therapy or phototherapy. [3] [4]

title: Deucravacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Deucravacitinib is a targeted treatment for patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis who qualify for systemic therapy or phototherapy. This medication selectively inhibits the tyrosine kinase 2 (Tyk2) protein, providing greater efficacy and a superior safety profile compared to other biological agents, particularly apremilast. Deucravacitinib may be administered orally once daily, which enhances patient compliance and improves treatment outcomes.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
content: Absorption: Following an oral dose of deucravacitinib, the Cmax and AUC increased proportionally, exhibiting linear pharmacokinetics. The absolute bioavailability of deucravacitinib was 99%, and the median Tmax was achieved in 2 to 3 hours. [22] The PK of deucravacitinib and its major active metabolite (BMT-153261) were almost comparable between healthy controls and patients with psoriasis. [3] A once-daily 6 mg dosing regimen resulted in a steady-state Cmax and AUC of deucravacitinib of 45 ng/mL and 473 ng/mL, respectively. The same dose resulted in a steady-state Cmax and AUC 24 of BMT-153261 of 5 ng/mL and 95 ng·hr/mL, respectively. [3] No significant differences were observed after a calorie-dense, high-fat meal of 51 total kcal with an approximate macronutrient distribution of 33% carbohydrates, 15% protein, and 52% fat. [3] When administered with food, the Cmax and AUC of deucravacitinib were reduced by approximately 24% and 11%, respectively, and the Tmax was prolonged by 1 hour. When administered with food, Cmax and AUC of BMT-153261 were reduced by approximately 23% and 10%, respectively, and the Tmax was prolonged by 2 hours.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
content: Older patients: In one clinical trial, 1519 patients with plaque psoriasis received deucravacitinib therapy. Of these, 152 patients (10%) were 65 or older, and 21 patients (1.4%) were 75. During the first 16 weeks of observation, severe adverse reactions occurred in 80 patients older than 65 and 12 patients older than 75; these patients were administered deucravacitinib without switching treatment arms. This rate of severe reactions was determined to be significantly higher than in younger patients. These severe effects included infection and many required treatment discontinuation. No significant difference in efficacy was observed between older and younger adults.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory disorder. The disease burden is equally distributed in both genders worldwide. This condition causes physical disfigurement, which has a profound psychosocial impact, leading to low self-esteem, stigmatization, and isolation. Additionally, it reduces the lifespan of patients due to associated comorbidities. The expensive and prolonged treatment imposes a substantial economic burden on the patient and healthcare system. The community needs safer and more effective treatment to address these issues. Deucravacitinib is a breakthrough in the targeted treatment of moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis. [3] The interdisciplinary healthcare team must work together to ensure the therapeutic safety and efficacy of deucravacitinib.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Monitoring
content: Patients on deucravacitinib require follow-up visits to evaluate their response to therapy. Close monitoring of the patients for prompt detection and effective management of adverse drug reactions such as infections, rhabdomyolysis, hepatic dysfunction, and hypertriglyceridemia is required. [24] In addition, patients are assessed for complications associated with psoriasis. At a dosage range up to 6 times the recommended daily dosage of 6 mg, deucravacitinib does not cause any clinically significant prolongation of QTc interval. [29] Patients receiving deucravacitinib therapy should be monitored using the following markers: Triglycerides Lipids Liver enzymes Creatine phosphokinase Glomerular filtration rate

title: Deucravacitinib -- Adverse Effects
content: Deucravacitinib has consistently demonstrated a safety profile. [3] The safety of deucravacitinib was assessed in 2 placebo-controlled and active-controlled (PSO-1 and PSO-2) clinical trials and an open-label-extension (OLE) trial in which patients could enroll after completing the PSO-1 or PSO-2 trials. A total of 1519 patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis received deucravacitinib 6 mg once daily. Of these, 1141 patients were exposed to deucravacitinib for at least 12 months.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
content: Jak inhibition: The involvement of Tyk2 in adverse reactions associated with Jak inhibitor therapy has not been well established.  One randomized post-marketing safety trial studied the effect of a Jak inhibitor in patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Along with at least one cardiovascular risk factor, higher rates of all-cause mortality were observed in patients aged 50 and older who received Jak inhibitor treatment compared to TNF blockers. All-cause mortality included sudden cardiovascular death, major cardiovascular adverse events, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, overall thrombosis, and malignancies (excluding non-melanomatous dermatological cancers).

title: Deucravacitinib -- Adverse Effects
content: At week 16 in the PSO-1 trial, significantly higher response rates were observed in patients receiving deucravacitinib compared to placebo or apremilast. Therapeutic efficacy improved with treatment longer than 16 weeks and was maintained through week 52. At week 16 in the PSO-2 trial, more than 50% of patients receiving deucravacitinib achieved superior clearing compared to apremilast. Across multiple efficacy endpoints, deucravacitinib consistently demonstrated superiority to apremilast and was well tolerated in patients with moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis. [25] A large proportion of patients treated with deucravacitinib reported a Psoriasis Symptoms and Signs Diary (PSSD) symptoms score of 0 at week 16, indicating relief from itching, pain, stinging, burning, and skin tightness (8% in deucravacitinib group versus 1% in the placebo arm) in both clinical trials. [26]

title: Deucravacitinib -- Mechanism of Action
content: Inhibition of Tyk2 promotes downregulation of the IL-23 (T H 17), type I interferon, IL-12, and keratinocyte activation pathways. However, the precise mechanism linking Tyk2 inhibition to the effective treatment of moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis is unknown. [3] In patients with psoriasis, deucravacitinib reduces the expression of psoriasis-associated genes in a dose-dependent fashion. This includes reducing type I IFN pathway-regulated genes and the IL-23 pathway. Deucravacitinib reduces IL-17A levels by 47% to 50%, IL-19 by 72%, and β-defensin by 81% to 84% after once-daily administration for 16 weeks. However, the mechanism linking these reduced inflammation marker levels to the clinical effects of deucravacitinib has not been established.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Mechanism of Action
content: Tyk2 promotes the signaling of inflammatory cytokines involved in innate (eg, type I interferons) and adaptive (eg, interleukins 12 and 23) immune responses. Interleukin 23 (IL-23) is associated with the pathogenesis of psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis. This cytokine initiates and stimulates the proliferation of T H 17 cells, which secrete inflammation mediators such as tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) and IL-17. These signal molecules stimulate epidermal cell production and secretion of cytokines and chemokines that recruit and activate cells of the innate immune system. This upregulated T H 17 cell activity causes sustained inflammation in the skin and joints, as seen in psoriatic arthritis.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
content: Malignancy: Various malignancies, including lymphomas, were reported in patients enrolled in clinical trials with deucravacitinib. [3] Clinicians should weigh the benefits versus risks for the patient before initiating or continuing treatment, particularly in patients with a known malignancy (other than resolved non-melanomatous dermatological cancers) and patients who develop a malignancy while receiving deucravacitinib therapy.

title: Plaque Psoriasis -- Treatment / Management -- Salicylic Acid
content: Acitretin is effective for keratoderma and pustular psoriasis phenotypes; however, its teratogenicity necessitates strict avoidance of pregnancy for up to three years following treatment. Oral alternatives include targeted small molecules such as apremilast—a PDE4 inhibitor—and JAK inhibitors, such as deucravacitinib and tofacitinib. Apremilast offers a favorable safety profile, primarily limited to gastrointestinal symptoms and weight loss, and requires minimal laboratory monitoring.

title: Biologic Response Modifiers (BRMs) -- Indications
content: TNF inhibitors, e.g., infliximab, etanercept, adalimumab, certolizumab, and golimumab has been FDA approved for use in RA, ulcerative colitis (UC), Crohn disease (CD), AS, JIA, plaque psoriasis and, psoriatic arthritis. Adalimumab also has approval for hidradenitis suppurativa and uveitis. Non-FDA-approved indications include pyoderma gangrenosum, pustular psoriasis, and GVHD(etanercept). Interleukin-1 inhibitor, e.g., anakinra, is FDA approved for RA and neonatal-onset multisystem inflammatory disease. IL-2 inhibitor, e.g., aldesleukin, is used in metastatic renal cell carcinoma and melanoma. Tocilizumab is an interleukin-6 inhibitor used in giant cell arteritis, JIA, and RA. Secukinumab that targets interleukin-17 is FDA approved for psoriasis (plaque and arthritis), and AS. Ustekinumab blocks interleukin 12/23 and has been FDA-approved for plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and CD. Abatacept (T-cell co-stimulation blocker) has approval for use in RA, JIA, and Psoriatic arthritis. Rituximab is FDA approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, non-Hodgkins lymphoma, RA, granulomatous polyangiitis, and pemphigus vulgaris. Off label uses include Burkitt lymphoma, CNS lymphoma, GVHD, lupus nephritis, and Hodgkin lymphoma. Tofacitinib is Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors indicated for psoriatic arthritis, RA, and UC. [11]

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
content: The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.
